judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 is more complete: it catches the same cross-tenant leak and correctly handles the SQL-injection non-issue like Report 1, but additionally identifies the nightly_export breakage in exports.py that Report 1 misses entirely.

report_1.evidence: the raw SQL built with `sqlalchemy.text(...)` only filters on `status` and the full-text match against `notes`; `tenant_id` is never referenced in the `WHERE` clause or bound as a parameter.

report_2.evidence: The function accepts `tenant_id` as a parameter but the raw SQL it builds only filters on `status` and the full-text match against `notes` — it never adds `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id`

The branch changes `build_appointment_query`'s signature from `(session, tenant_id)` to `(session, tenant_id, filters)` with `filters` as a required positional parameter (no default). `backend/app/exports.py`'s `nightly_export` was not updated and still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with two arguments. This will raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'` the next time the nightly per-tenant CSV export runs

report_1.evidence: Today this is not exploitable for injection because `status` is constrained by FastAPI to the `AppointmentStatus` enum before it reaches this function ... so `status.value` can only ever be one of `"booked"/"cancelled"/"completed"`. I'm flagging this as a secondary/lower-severity code-quality and defense-in-depth issue rather than a standalone blocker ... but on its own, given the current call site, it doesn't produce an exploitable defect.

report_2.evidence: `status` is constrained by the `AppointmentStatus` enum at the FastAPI layer so this particular field isn't currently injectable, but it's an inconsistent, risky pattern ... (Minor, non-blocking but worth flagging)
